metadata.title: God and Argument.
metadata.description: The article presents the author's views on debates on God's existence as of 2015 with regard to why they have persisted and have not been concluded, highlighting philosopher Paul Moser's opinion on the issue. He states that there have been numerous debates on God's existence which is the most significant philosophical issue. Two cynical theories on the issue are explored as well as the arguments of theists and atheists. Other topics include the argument that God is worthy of worship.

metadata.title: To “Let Religion Itself Speak:” Feuerbach and Religious Consciousness in Modern Culture
metadata.description: The article presents the author's views on philosopher Ludwig Feuerbach's opinion on religious conciousness in modern culture. Feuerbach's model of religious consciousness is explored which reportedly also demonstrates self-consciousness based on religious feeling. Other topics include the book

metadata.title: Dispositions, Habits and Virtues: Comments on Mulder and Kelly.
metadata.description: The article presents the author's views on dispositions, habits and virtues, highlighting the essays by Jack Mulder on philosopher Soren Kierkegaard's heritage on continental philosophy and Michael Kelly on the differences between emulation and envy. Also discussed are philosopher Aristotle's beliefs on habit and virtue, Mulder's claim that love is not a static property, and Kierkegaard's argument that love is unconditional.

metadata.title: Faith: Belief or Practice?
metadata.description: The article presents the author's views on faith with regard to whether it is belief or practice and situates it within the philosophy of religion. The differences between analytical and continental methods of philosophical analysis are discussed as well as faith as a social phenomenon. Other topics include religious language, the conflict between faith and reason, and the features of saintliness.

metadata.title: Meeting Mary in Myth: Pursuing Pre/Post/Modern Apologetics.
metadata.description: The article presents the author's views on apologetics which is a sub-field of the philosophy of religion. Apologetics refer to writings that justify something or to reasoned arguments. The views of author C. S. Lewis on Christianity's division is explored as well as continental and analytical philosophies of religion. Other topics include philosopher John Caputo's views on the relationship between analytic and continental philosophy.

metadata.title: Humanizing Philosophy of Religion: on Language in Levinas and Sellars.
metadata.description: An essay is presented on the philosophy of religion (PoR) with regard to two orientations to the subject. These orientations are the claim that the PoR's purpose is to promote a set of absolute moral norms and the claim that the field aims to determine whether religious communities' practices are true. Also explored are the tendency of the PoR to enable a thinking subject to make true claims, the mashup PoR, and the canon of modern PoR.

metadata.title: The Object and Affects of Envy and Emulation.
metadata.description: An essay is presented on the relevance of envy and emulation in the philosophy of religion. It examines virtues to determine the impact of right and wrong perceptions on belief and practice. The subjective dimensions of envy and emulation are explored based on philosopher Aristotle's views on both emotions. Other topics include analytic and continental philosophy, the gap between virtue and vice and the differences between envy and emulation.

metadata.title: Is Mashup the Right Metaphor?
metadata.description: The article examines mashups as a metaphor for conceptualizing philosophy of religion (PoR). The limitations of the mashup metaphor is explored as well as a critical inquiry method neglected by the mashup metaphor. It offers information on mashup music and proposes a metaphor that fosters productive thinking of PoR. Other topics include the diversity of religious phenomena, the analytic and continental modes of inquiry, and modern PoR.

metadata.title: Prolegomena to Any Future Mashups with the Philosophy of Religion.
metadata.description: An essay is presented on modern English-language philosophy of religion (PoR) with regard to its possible mashup with other disciplines and as a specialized discussion within philosophy. Topics include critical thinking, a mousetrap analogy that supports the theory that a theistic God exists, and the need for PoR to be transformed and reject classical theism. Also discussed are typical introductory religious studies classroom and introductory approaches of books and scholarly journals.

metadata.title: God and Epistemic Authority.
metadata.description: The article presents the author's views on God and epistemic authority. He explores human beings' fabrication of gods for various reasons which include to reflect their imperfect traits. God's epistemic authority is discussed as well as how it can benefit an epistemiology of people's understanding of God. Other topics include epistemic idolatry, curative knowledge, and God's characterstics.

metadata.title: Knowledge, Virtue, and Ontotheology: a Kierkegaardian (Self-)Critique.
metadata.description: The article examines philosopher Soren Kierkegaarde's views on faith with regard to ontotheology, virtue, and knowledge. Several methods used by Kierkegaarde to discuss faith as an ethical state are explored to illustrate that they can be examined based on the virtue tradition. Also discussed are the continental tradition in philosophy, the philosophy of religion (PoR), and mashup PoR.

metadata.title: A Shallow Species in Deep Time: Modernism, Postmodernism, and Our Place in the Potential History of Inquiry.
metadata.description: The article examines the role of deep time in motivating epistemic humility with regard to humans' place in time. Modernism and postmodernism are discussed which are two forms of the philosophy and situates them within the philosophy of religion. An overview of deep time is provided and explores the positive and negative aspects of modernism and post modernism. Also tackled are pessimism and optimism, intellectual humility and consilience, and continental and analytic religious philosophy.

metadata.title: The Weird Place of God Debates in the Academic Study of Religions.
metadata.description: The article presents the author's views on the debates about God's existence in the academic study of religions, highlighting the possible mash-ups between the philosophy of religion and other disciplines. He critiques the articles by Scott F. Aikin and Paul K. Moser on the philosophy of religion which are published within the issue. Also discussed are the advantages and disadvantages of mash-ups.

metadata.title: On the Ethical Basis of Language: Some Themes in Davidson, Cavell, and Levinas.
metadata.description: An essay is presented on the views of three philosophers on the relationship between being a linguistic agent and an ethical agent with regard to the philosophy of religion. These philosophers are Donald Davidson, Stanley Cavell, and Emmanuel Levinas. Other topics include how understanding language needs community conceptualization, the relationship between language and ethics, and skepticism.

metadata.title: Introduction: the Dialogical Promise of Mashup Philosophy of Religion.
metadata.description: An introduction is presented in which the editor discusses various reports within the issue on topics including the concept of mashup philosophy of religion, mashup music, and the challlenges that mashup philosophy might face as of March 2015.

metadata.title: Philosophy Mashed-up and in Crisis.
metadata.description: The article presents the author's views on the meta-philosophy of mash-up philosophy of religion (PoR) based on the concept of mashup music. He explores his experience of undertaking a meta-philosophical journey as well as the failure of mash-up philosophy to address issues facing philosophy. Other topics include the auditing culture, the use of neoliberal principles in higher education, and the need for modern philosophers to practice outide conventional institutional boundaries.

metadata.title: Philiosophy Mashed Up and In Crisis
Vernacular Fundamentalism After ŽIŽEk: Some Ethical Concerns About Religious Online Communication.
metadata.description: An essay is presented on philosopher Slavoj Žižek's promotion of the need to examine the ethical aspects of religious online communication and on professor Robert Glenn Howard's case study, Digital Jesus. It compares both philosophers views on religious online communication and examines the role mashup philosophy of religion on the use of religious language in faith and politics. Also discussed is the nexus between immoralism, extremism, literalism, and dogmatism.
